While Evers agrees there needs to be better protections in place for kids, he said the bill was intrusive to personal privacy. Jacque disagrees with the governor's assessment. He said this legislation was the perfect opportunity to join 25 other states with similar laws in place. Evers said a better solution would be to work with tech companies on device-based age verification. Jacque called this a cop-out and believes there would be too many loopholes users could jump through to evade the law. It's unclear if either of these bills or similar ones will make a return in the future. The district says once a student reaches $10 in lunch debt, they will be given an alternate entree. Students still get a full meal, but instead of the entree of the day, they'll get a cheese or peanut butter sandwich. They still get everything else like fruits, vegetables and milk. Lorionna Metzger, the executive director of Lojjaden Farms, wanted to do something to help those students out. After all, Metzger says there's shaming that goes along with the alternate lunch. "It's an embarrassing thing for kids to be standing in those lunch lines and not have money to pay. It's a very meager lunch. So, when a kid comes to the table with that tray, it's very obvious what's going on. There's a social stigma that goes with being in that category," Metzger said. Metzger has created a GoFundMe page to help raise money to pay off all the student debt. "This community has the ability to solve this problem for the entire district very, very easily. I've seen this community come together for fundraisers for things not as important as things like this," Metzger said. The GoFundMe has raised more than $300 in its first day. "If you kind of break it down by school, that could solve the lunch debt for two of the elementary schools right away," Metzger said. School cafeterias are funded through federal reimbursements and money from what is sold. Districts are not allowed to use those federal dollars to cover lunch debt. The money would have to come from the district's general fund. If you do donate, because Lojjaden Farms is a nonprofit educational farm, your donation is tax-deductible. Milford Schools did not ask for anyone to help with the school lunch debt.

cont_clean: #5 - Women's empowerment in a context of free reproductive healthcare and family planning in rural.. Women's empowerment in a context of free reproductive healthcare and family planning in rural Burkina Faso Camille Beaujoin, Alice Bila, Frank Bicaba, Véronique Plouffe, Abel Bicaba, Thomas Druetz University of Montreal, School of Public Health ; Société d'Études et de Recherche en Santé Publique (SERSAP) Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso PURPOSE: In Burkina Faso, the national user fee exemption policy aims to reduce maternal mortality by improving access to reproductive health and family planning services. This policy has helped to alleviate the financial barrier, however, other obstacles still limit women's access to care, such as their lack of decision-making power regarding health matters. The objective of our study is to describe the decision-making processes that take place within households regarding access to reproductive health care and family planning. These decision-making processes have not yet been studied in the context of free health care in Burkina Faso. METHODS: We conducted a descriptive qualitative study. Qualitative data were collected through semi-structured interviews and focus groups with Burkinabe women of childbearing age living in rural areas (n = 23), as well as their husbands (n = 8), and representatives of local women's organizations as key informants (n = 4). Recruitment and data collection took place in four Health and Social Promotion Centers (CSPS) located in the Cascades and Center-West regions of Burkina Faso. Several major themes were discussed with participants, such as family relationships, decision-making processes in daily life and regarding access to reproductive healthcare, childcare, and family planning, and the influence of the user fee exemption policy on women's decision-making power. These data were analyzed according to the principle of thematic analysis using open coding. OUTCOMES: Our results show that the national user fee exemption policy appears to be effective in improving women's access to reproductive health and family planning services by facilitating the negotiation processes that take place between women and their families within households. Lowering the financial barrier allows women to access reproductive health services without having to negotiate for the necessary financial resources. However, the weight of social norms and gender inequalities may still limit women's decision-making power in accessing care, as women often still need to obtain permission from their husbands or in-laws before joining health services despite the introduction of the national user fee exemption policy. In light of these results, courses of action should be implemented in addition to the user fee exemption policy to improve women's empowerment regarding reproductive health, and in particular with regard to family planning, in rural areas of Burkina Faso. I would like to thank the participants, as well as the research team of the Société d'Études et de Recherche en Santé Publique (SERSAP) who made this project possible. This work was carried out with the aid of a grant from the Innovating for Maternal and Child Health in Africa initiative- a partnership of Global Affairs Canada (GAC), the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR) and Canada's International Development Research Centre (IDRC). For more information or if you have questions, feel free to contact me at camille.beaujoin@umontreal.ca (in English or French).
